pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) THE SMOKING GUN: The constellation of severe, inexplicable abdominal pain coupled with rapid onset polyneuropathy, autonomic instability (tachycardia/hypertension), hyponatremia, and neuropsychiatric crisis, all exacerbated by the initiation of a P450-inducing medication (lorazepam in this case). Confirmation relies on quantitative testing showing significantly elevated urinary Porphobilinogen (PBG) and Delta-Aminolevulinic acid (ALA). PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: AIP is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by a partial deficiency of the enzyme Hydroxymethylbilane Synthase (HMBS), also known as Porphobilinogen Deaminase (PBGD), the third enzyme in the heme biosynthesis pathway. This deficiency usually remains dormant until precipitated by environmental triggers (e.g., fasting/dieting, stress, infection, or exposure to certain drugs like barbiturates, sulfonamides, or some benzodiazepines like lorazepam, which induce hepatic CYP enzymes). These triggers increase the demand for hepatic heme, overwhelming the partially deficient enzyme system. This leads to the massive accumulation of the neurotoxic heme precursors, ALA and PBG, which directly affect the central, peripheral, and autonomic nervous systems, resulting in the characteristic abdominal crises, neuropathy, psychiatric disturbances, and severe hypertension/tachycardia. The dark urine is due to the spontaneous oxidation of PBG to porphobilin.
